Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside The Default Judgment
Off Calendar Apr-05-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. RAQUEL MOR, Esq. of Michael Sullivan & Associates LLP (tel: 714-202-3440) for Plaintiff.
Atty. MARIA SOLOMON-WILLIAMS, Esq. of Wood, Smith, Henning & Berman LLP (tel: 707-559-6750) for Defendant.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT JH CONSTRUCTION INC.'s Motion To Set Aside The Default Judgment.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant JH Construction Inc.'s motion "to set aside the default and any judgment thereon" is denied. (Notice of Motion, 1:25.)

Defendant's motion for relief pursuant to CCP 473(b) is denied. Under that provision, a party may move for discretionary relief where a default is entered due to "mistake, inadvertence, surprise or excusable neglect." The discretionary application "shall be made within a reasonable time, in no case exceeding six months, after the proceeding was taken." In this case, the proceeding was the entry of the default. Defendant's motion is untimely since the default was entered on May 30, 2023, and defendant filed this motion on February 22, 2024. (See Pulte Homes Corp. v. Williams Mechanical, Inc. (2016) 2 Cal.App.5th 267, 273 [holding that the trial court "could not set aside the default" under section 473(b) where the motion "was filed less than six months after entry of default judgment, but more than six months after entry of default"]; Rappleyea v. Campbell (1994) 8 Cal.4th 975, 980 ["more than six months had elapsed from the entry of default, and hence relief under section 473 was unavailable"].)

Defendant refers to other inapposite provisions to obtain relief. CCP 473(b) permits mandatory relief where the application is accompanied by an attorney affidavit of fault. Relief can be obtained if the motion is filed "no more than six months after entry of judgment." (Id.) That timing provision does not apply because defendant does not proffer an attorney affidavit of fault. Defendant further refers to the timing provisions of CCP 473.5. That section is irrelevant because defendant did not lack "actual notice" of the action. (CCP 473.5(a).) Defendant concedes proper service of the complaint. (Huerta Decl., par. 2.)

Defendant notes that plaintiff never filed and served a notice of entry of default. That issue is important to the timing requirements of CCP 473.5 but as noted, that provision is inapt. Defendant cites no authority holding that the failure to serve the notice of entry of default has any bearing on the timing requirements of CCP 473 [discretionary relief]. Indeed, plaintiff served a request for entry of default on defendant in compliance with CCP 587 and defendant should have been on notice of the impending default.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Rochelle C. East; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RCE)
